title: Applied Mycology & Agricultural Botany
subject: Bio-Sciences & Mycology
description: Study fungal life cycles, sterile liquid culture inoculation, substrate pasteurization, and greenhouse climate controls.
learningObjectives: Trace the fungal life cycle from spore germination to hyphae, mycelium, and fruiting body.
Perform sterile grain inoculations using still air boxes (SAB) or HEPA laminar flow hoods.
Formulate woodchip and grain substrates for Lion's Mane, Shiitake, and Oyster mushrooms.

lessons.title: The Fungal Kingdom & Decomposition Physics
lessons.content: Unlike plants, fungi do not synthesize energy via photosynthesis. They are saprophytes, secreting extracellular enzymes (ligninases and cellulases) to break down complex organic polymers into bio-available carbohydrates.

lessons.title: Greenhouse Climate Parameters
lessons.content: Fruiting requires precise environmental triggers: Relative Humidity (85-95%), Fresh Air Exchange (CO2 < 800 ppm), and ambient temperature (18-24°C / 65-75°F).

flashcards.definition: The vegetative network of fungal threads (hyphae) living within substrate.
Heating substrate to 65-80°C for 1-2 hours to kill competing organisms while retaining beneficial microflora.
An organism that feeds on decaying organic matter.

questions.question: What extracellular enzymes do wood-decay fungi secrete to break down dense wood logs?
Why is Fresh Air Exchange (FAE) critical during the mushroom fruiting phase?
options: To raise CO2 levels to 5000 ppm
To lower CO2 concentrations so stems do not stretch long and thin
To dry out the substrate completely

practicalChallenge.title: Substrate Hydration & Field Capacity Test
practicalChallenge.instruction: Hydrate hardwood sawdust or straw. Squeeze a handful: exactly a few drops of water should squeeze through your knuckles (field capacity!).
